questions.question_text: Where must a DC broker maintain trust account funds?
questions.correct_answer: A federally insured institution in the District
distractors: Any bank in the Washington metro area
A personal savings account with proper records
A financial institution in any US state
questions.explanation: DC requires trust funds to be held in a separate trust or escrow account at a federally insured financial institution located in the District of Columbia.

questions.question_text: A buyer submits a $10,000 earnest money deposit. The contract does not specify a deposit deadline. When must the broker deposit it?
distractors: Within 24 hours of receipt
Within 3 business days of receipt
By the next business day
questions.explanation: DC requires trust funds to be deposited within the time specified in the contract. If no time is specified, the funds must be deposited within a reasonable time.

questions.question_text: Can a DC broker keep any personal funds in the trust account?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, a small amount to cover bank fees
distractors: No, absolutely no personal funds allowed
Yes, up to $500 for operating expenses
Yes, up to 10% of the account balance
questions.explanation: Brokers may maintain a small amount of personal funds in the trust account solely to cover bank service charges. This prevents the account from being closed due to fees depleting client funds.

questions.question_text: Under what circumstances may a DC broker disburse trust account funds?
questions.correct_answer: Only as authorized by the parties or by law
distractors: At the broker's discretion at any time
Only after the transaction has fully closed
Only with written approval from DLCP
questions.explanation: Trust funds may only be disbursed as authorized by the parties to the transaction or as required by contract or law. Unauthorized disbursement is a serious violation.

questions.question_text: A purchase contract specifies that the earnest money deposit must be made within 5 business days. The broker deposits it on day 7. What violation has occurred?
distractors: Commingling of client funds
Conversion of trust funds
No violation since it was deposited
questions.explanation: When the contract specifies a deposit timeline, the broker must comply. Late deposit violates the contract terms and DC trust account rules, though it is not commingling or conversion.

questions.question_text: What is commingling in the context of DC real estate trust accounts?
questions.correct_answer: Mixing client funds with personal or business funds
distractors: Holding funds from multiple clients together
Depositing funds in a non-DC bank
Earning interest on client trust funds
questions.explanation: Commingling occurs when a broker mixes client trust funds with their own personal or business operating funds. Holding multiple clients' funds together in one trust account is standard practice, not commingling.

questions.question_text: A DC broker uses a client's earnest money deposit to pay the office rent. This is an example of:
distractors: Commingling
Negligent accounting
An escrow shortage
questions.explanation: Conversion is the unauthorized use of client funds for personal or business purposes. It is more serious than commingling because the funds are actually spent rather than just improperly mixed.

questions.question_text: What is the potential consequence for a DC licensee found guilty of converting trust funds?
distractors: A written warning for the first offense
A mandatory 30-day suspension only
A fine of up to $500
questions.explanation: Conversion of trust funds is one of the most serious violations in real estate. It can result in license revocation, criminal prosecution, and civil liability. It demonstrates a fundamental breach of fiduciary duty.

questions.question_text: A broker deposits a client's earnest money into the firm's general operating account instead of the trust account. Even if the funds are not spent, this violates which rule?
distractors: The conversion prohibition only
Record retention requirements
The advertising rules
questions.explanation: Depositing client funds into any account other than the designated trust account constitutes commingling, even if the funds are never used for personal purposes. The violation is in the mixing, not the spending.

questions.question_text: Which statement about DC trust account rules is correct?
questions.correct_answer: Brokers cannot mix client funds with business funds
distractors: Salespersons may hold their own trust accounts
Trust accounts can be at any US bank
Interest on trust accounts goes to the broker
questions.explanation: Commingling is strictly prohibited in DC. Only brokers (not salespersons) can hold trust accounts, and they must be at federally insured institutions in DC.

questions.question_text: Who bears ultimate responsibility for trust account activity in a DC brokerage?
distractors: The salesperson who received the deposit
The office manager
DLCP
questions.explanation: The broker is responsible for all trust account activity, even if day-to-day management is delegated to an employee. The broker cannot shift liability by delegating the task.

questions.question_text: A broker delegates trust account management to an office administrator. If the administrator makes an error, who is responsible?
distractors: The administrator is solely responsible
Responsibility is shared equally
DLCP assumes responsibility for errors
questions.explanation: Even when trust account duties are delegated, the broker remains fully responsible for all trust account activity. Delegation does not transfer legal liability.

questions.question_text: DLCP has the authority to do what regarding a broker's trust account?
distractors: Freeze the account without notice
Transfer funds to the Guaranty Fund
Close the account and redistribute funds
questions.explanation: DLCP has the authority to audit trust account records to ensure compliance. Trust account records must be maintained and available for inspection. Freezing or closing accounts would require a court order.

questions.question_text: How long must a DC broker maintain trust account records?
questions.correct_answer: 3 years from closing or listing termination
distractors: 1 year from the date of deposit
5 years from the transaction date
7 years as required by the IRS
questions.explanation: DC requires brokers to maintain all transaction records, including trust account records, for 3 years from the date of closing or termination of the listing agreement.

questions.question_text: A broker's trust account is found to have a shortage during a DLCP audit. What is the broker's immediate obligation?
questions.correct_answer: Restore the account to the correct balance
distractors: File a report with the DC Council
Close the account and open a new one
Notify all clients of the shortage
questions.explanation: The broker must immediately restore the trust account to its correct balance. The shortage itself may trigger further investigation and potential disciplinary action by the Commission.
